claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 2 children:
  - Alex is 6 years old and lives with you.
  - Blake is 4 years old and does not live with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 6 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 4 (under 16)
  - Child does not live with claimant and weekly upkeep (£20.00) is below the Child Benefit rate (£26.05)
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 6 (under 16); Child lives with claimant; Child is not in local authority care; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Blake
expected_eligibility.reason: Child does not live with claimant and weekly upkeep (£20.00) is below the Child Benefit rate (£26.05)
circumstances: Child does not live with claimant and weekly upkeep (£20.00) is below the Child Benefit rate (£26.05)
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'll help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. Let me start the assessment process.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Let's start by understanding your situation. To determine eligibility for Child Benefit, I need to ask about the child (or children) you're claiming for.

**First question: How old is your child?**

This is important because:
- Children **under 16** are generally automatically qualifying
- Children **aged 16-19** may qualify if they're in approved education or training
- Children **aged 20 or over** typically don't qualify

Please tell me:
1. How old is your child?
2. If you have multiple children, please share their ages and names so I can assess each one

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text:
Hi! Thanks for helping me with this. I have two children I'd like to ask about.

My son Alex is 6 years old, and my daughter Blake is 4. They're both pretty young, so I'm hoping that makes things straightforward!

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! Yes, having younger children does make things more straightforward. Let me assess the eligibility for both Alex (age 6) and Blake (age 4).

Since both Alex and Blake are under 16, they fall into the automatic qualification age category. Let me continue with the next check.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect! Now the next question:

**Are Alex and Blake working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?**

Given that Alex is 6 and Blake is 4, I'm assuming they're not in paid work at all, but I need to confirm this to proceed with the assessment. Can you confirm that neither of them is working?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Oh no, neither of them is working at all! They're far too young for that - Alex is in primary school and Blake is still in nursery. So yes, they're definitely not doing any paid work.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent, thank you for confirming! That's what I expected given their ages. Let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Next question:

**Are Alex and Blake NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right?**

Specifically, I'm asking whether either of them receives benefits like Employment and Support Allowance (ESA) or Universal Credit in their own name (not as part of your household claim, but in their own right).

Given their young ages (6 and 4), I expect the answer is that they're NOT receiving these benefits, but I need to confirm. Can you confirm that neither Alex nor Blake receives benefits in their own right?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: That's right - neither Alex nor Blake receives any benefits in their own name. They're just little kids, so they don't have anything like that in their own right.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect, thank you for confirming! Let me continue to the next check.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Excellent! Next question:

**Are Alex and Blake NOT in any apprenticeships?**

This question relates to whether they're in certain types of apprenticeships that could disqualify them (such as English apprenticeships, Scottish Modern Apprenticeships, etc.).

Given that Alex is 6 and Blake is 4, they're clearly far too young to be in any apprenticeship program. Can you confirm that neither of them is in an apprenticeship?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, definitely not - they're way too young for apprenticeships! Alex is only 6 and Blake is 4, so neither of them is doing anything like that.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Absolutely, that makes perfect sense! Let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Now we need to check your residency status. This is an important requirement for Child Benefit.

**Do you meet the UK residency requirements?**

You can qualify through ANY of these paths:

1. **You normally live in the UK** (this is the most common route)
2. You have settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme
3. You have pre-settled status and are working (earning above the National Insurance threshold for 3 continuous months)
4. You have pre-settled status and are actively seeking work (up to 91 days)
5. You have pre-settled status with sufficient resources to support your family
6. You have pre-settled status as a student with sufficient resources
7. You have pre-settled status as a family member of an EEA/Swiss national with right to reside
8. You're living abroad as a Crown servant or in certain qualifying countries

**Can you tell me about your residency situation?** Do you normally live in the UK, or does one of the other situations apply to you?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I normally live in the UK. I live in Manchester with Alex, so that should be straightforward.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! Living normally in the UK is the most straightforward path and you meet the residency requirement. Let me continue with the next check.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Excellent! Now I need to check about your responsibility for the children.

**Are you responsible for Alex and Blake?**

You meet this requirement through ANY of these paths:

1. **You live with the child** (most common)
2. You contribute to their upkeep (paying at least the same amount as Child Benefit towards their care - food, clothes, housing, etc.)
3. You pay maintenance under a court order or agreement

You mentioned you live in Manchester with Alex. **Can you confirm:**
- Do you live with both Alex (6) and Blake (4)?
- Are they both living with you in your household?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: I live with Alex, yes - he's at home with me in Manchester. But Blake doesn't live with me. Blake lives with their other parent. I do help out a bit with things for Blake though - I contribute about £20 a week towards clothes and things like that.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for clarifying that. Let me get some important information about the Child Benefit amounts to assess Blake's situation properly.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
I can see that the specific payment rates aren't listed in the constants, but let me check the current rates. According to current UK government guidance, Child Benefit is approximately:
- **£25.60 per week** for the eldest/only child
- **£16.95 per week** for each additional child

Now, let me assess your situation for both children:

**For Alex (6 years old):**
- You live with Alex in Manchester ✓
- This meets the responsibility requirement

**For Blake (4 years old):**
- Blake lives with their other parent (not with you)
- You contribute £20 per week towards clothes and things
- However, Blake would be considered an "additional child" (since Alex is older), so the Child Benefit rate for Blake would be approximately £16.95 per week
- Your £20/week contribution exceeds this amount ✓

So it appears you meet the responsibility requirement for both children! Let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Important question now:

**Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex or Blake?**

This is particularly important for Blake since they live with their other parent. 

- Is Blake's other parent currently claiming Child Benefit for Blake?
- Is anyone claiming Child Benefit for Alex?

Please let me know the situation for both children.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text:
No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex - he lives with me and I'm the one looking after him.

But yes, Blake's other parent is already claiming Child Benefit for Blake since Blake lives with them.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that important information. This creates a significant issue for Blake's eligibility.

**Here's the situation:**

Since Blake's other parent is already claiming Child Benefit for Blake, **only one person can claim Child Benefit for the same child at any given time**. 

There are a few possible scenarios:

1. **No dispute - other parent continues claiming:** Blake's other parent keeps the claim (you cannot claim for Blake)

2. **Agreed transfer:** If you and Blake's other parent both agree that YOU should claim instead, then the other parent would need to stop their claim and you could take over

3. **Disputed claim:** If both of you want to claim and cannot agree, HMRC would have to decide who has priority (usually the person the child lives with has priority)

**Can you tell me:**
- Do you want to claim Child Benefit for Blake, or are you happy for the other parent to continue claiming since Blake lives with them?
- If you do want to claim for Blake, has the other parent agreed to transfer the claim to you?

This will determine whether we proceed with Blake's assessment or just continue with Alex's.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Oh, I see. No, I'm happy for Blake's other parent to keep claiming the Child Benefit since Blake lives with them. That makes sense. I just wanted to check if I could claim for Alex since he lives with me.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect, that makes complete sense! So we'll continue the assessment just for **Alex**, who lives with you in Manchester.

Since no one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex, let me continue.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Good! Now I need to check Alex's living situation to see if any special rules apply.

**Which of these describes Alex's current living situation?**

- Does Alex live normally with you at home in Manchester?
- Is Alex currently in hospital?
- Is Alex in local authority care?
- Is Alex in a fostering arrangement?

Given what you've told me (that Alex lives at home with you and is in primary school), I'm assuming he's living normally with you, but I need to confirm this. Can you confirm Alex's living situation?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, that's right - Alex lives normally at home with me in Manchester. He's not in hospital or care or anything like that. He just goes to primary school during the day and lives at home with me.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! That's straightforward. Let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! One final question:

**Is the local council NOT paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance?**

In other words, are you (rather than the local council) responsible for paying for Alex's accommodation and maintenance costs?

Given that Alex lives with you at home and you're looking after him, I expect the answer is that the council is NOT paying for him, but I need to confirm this. Can you confirm?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: That's correct - the council isn't paying anything towards Alex. I'm the one paying for everything - his accommodation, food, clothes, all of that. It's all coming from me.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! That's exactly what I needed to confirm. Let me now proceed to the final outcome.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
## 🎉 Great News! You ARE Eligible for Child Benefit for Alex!

### **Summary of Your Assessment:**

**For Alex (age 6):** ✅ **ELIGIBLE**
- Alex is under 16 years old
- He's not in paid work
- He's not receiving benefits in his own right
- He's not in an apprenticeship
- You live with him in the UK (Manchester)
- You're responsible for his care
- No one else is claiming Child Benefit for him
- He lives normally at home with you
- The local council is not paying towards his care

**For Blake (age 4):** ❌ **NOT ELIGIBLE for you to claim**
- Blake's other parent is already claiming Child Benefit for Blake
- Only one person can claim Child Benefit for the same child at a time
- Since Blake lives with their other parent and you're happy for them to continue claiming, that arrangement will stay in place

### **Next Steps to Claim for Alex:**

1. **Apply online or by post** using form CH2
2. **Provide proof of identity** for yourself and Alex
3. **Provide Alex's birth certificate** or adoption certificate
4. **Be aware of the High Income Child Benefit Charge:** If you or your partner earns over £60,000 per year, you may need to pay back some or all of the Child Benefit through your tax return
5. **Payments:** Child Benefit is usually paid every 4 weeks (or weekly if you're a single parent or receiving certain benefits)

### **How to Apply:**
Visit: https://www.gov.uk/child-benefit/how-to-claim

Do you have any questions about the application process or anything else?

reasonings: Alex is 6 years old (under 16), not in paid work, not receiving benefits in his own right, not in an apprenticeship, lives with the claimant in the UK (Manchester), the claimant is responsible for him, no one else is claiming Child Benefit for him, he lives normally at home, and the local council is not paying towards his accommodation or maintenance. Alex meets all Child Benefit eligibility requirements.

Blake is 4 years old and would otherwise qualify based on age and other criteria. However, Blake lives with their other parent who is already claiming Child Benefit for Blake. Only one person can claim Child Benefit for the same child at any given time. The claimant has confirmed they are happy for Blake's other parent to continue claiming since Blake lives with them. Therefore, the claimant is not eligible to claim Child Benefit for Blake.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant has two children: Alex (6) and Blake (4). Alex lives with the claimant in Manchester and meets all eligibility criteria. Blake lives with their other parent who is already claiming Child Benefit, and the claimant has agreed to let that continue. The claimant is eligible to claim Child Benefit for Alex only.

response.question: Is the child NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right (e.g., ESA, Universal Credit)?
response.help_text: Children receiving Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit in their own right are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT in a disqualifying apprenticeship?
response.help_text: Regional rules apply: English apprenticeships (all types) disqualify. Scottish Modern Apprenticeships disqualify. Welsh Foundation Apprenticeships are APPROVED. Other Welsh apprenticeships disqualify.

response.question: Does the claimant meet UK residency requirements?
response.help_text: Must normally live in the UK or meet special circumstances
